name: network-netcat
description: Network utility for reading and writing data across TCP/UDP connections, port scanning, file transfers, and backdoor communication channels. Use when: (1) Testing network connectivity and port availability, (2) Creating reverse shells and bind shells for authorized penetration testing, (3) Transferring files between systems in restricted environments, (4) Banner grabbing and service enumeration, (5) Establishing covert communication channels, (6) Testing firewall rules and network segmentation.
capabilities: Documentation for TCP/UDP network connections and testing
Port scanning and connectivity testing techniques
Banner grabbing and service enumeration procedures
Reverse shell and bind shell establishment guidance
Relay and pivoting techniques for network movement
author: AgentSecOps
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill providing educational content for authorized penetration testing. Contains no executable code, scripts, or malicious payloads. All content explicitly requires written authorization and includes cleanup guidance. Static findings are false positives - the analyzer detected command examples in documentation, not actual code execution.
valueStatement: Security professionals need documented guidance on using netcat for authorized penetration testing, port scanning, file transfers, and reverse shell establishment. This skill provides comprehensive workflows and command references for offensive security operations with explicit authorization requirements.
userTitle: Use netcat for network security testing
limitations: Documentation only - provides no automated script execution
Requires external netcat installation (netcat or ncat packages)
All operations require explicit written authorization
Does not include actual network exploitation tools

useCases.title: Authorized testing operations
useCases.description: Use documented netcat techniques during authorized red team engagements with proper scoping documentation.

useCases.title: Network security assessment
useCases.description: Test network connectivity, port availability, and firewall rules during security assessments.

useCases.title: Threat detection research
useCases.description: Understand attacker techniques to develop detection and monitoring capabilities for defensive security.

promptTemplates.title: Basic connectivity test
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to use netcat to test if port 80 is open on target 192.168.1.100 and verify HTTP service availability.

promptTemplates.title: Banner grabbing
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I grab the SSH or SMTP banner from a target system using netcat for service enumeration?

promptTemplates.title: File transfer
promptTemplates.prompt: Document the commands to transfer a file from one system to another using netcat when standard methods are blocked.

promptTemplates.title: Authorized shell
promptTemplates.prompt: Show netcat reverse shell techniques with proper cleanup procedures for authorized penetration testing only.

bestPractices: Verify written authorization before any network testing
Document all activities including timestamps, IPs, ports, and commands executed
Clean up all shells, listeners, and persistence mechanisms after engagement completion
antiPatterns: Using netcat techniques without proper written authorization
Failing to document penetration testing activities and findings
Leaving persistence mechanisms (cron jobs, services) active after engagement completion

faq.question: Is netcat installed by default on systems?
faq.answer: Netcat is not always pre-installed. Install with apt-get install netcat-traditional or ncat for enhanced SSL features.

faq.question: What is the difference between nc and ncat?
faq.answer: ncat (from Nmap) offers SSL encryption, connection relaying, and HTTP proxy support. Use ncat when encryption is required.

faq.question: Does this skill execute any code on systems?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides documentation and command references only. It does not contain or execute any scripts or payloads.

faq.question: Can I use these techniques on any network without permission?
faq.answer: No. All techniques require explicit written authorization. Unauthorized network access is illegal and unethical.

faq.question: How do I detect netcat activity defensively?
faq.answer: Monitor for nc/ncat process execution, -e flag usage, unusual outbound connections, and named pipe creation (mkfifo).

faq.question: What security frameworks does this skill reference?
faq.answer: MITRE ATT&CK techniques T1059 (Command Shell), T1071 (Application Layer Protocol), T1090 (Proxy), and T1105 (Ingress Tool Transfer).
